title: Brooklyn Pastor Paul Mitchell Pleads Guilty to Tax Evasion After Using Church Funds as Personal Luxury Account
teaser: Paul Mitchell, lead pastor of a Brooklyn church and president of its affiliated daycare, pleaded guilty to tax evasion after spending years charging personal luxuries to his organizations' credit cards. The items purchased included men's clothing, jewelry, luxury watches, and life insurance premiums. Federal prosecutors say he stole millions from the very congregation he was called to serve.
body:
There is a long theological tradition, across virtually every major faith, of distinguishing between what belongs to the flock and what belongs to the shepherd. Paul Mitchell, lead pastor of a Brooklyn church and president of its church-affiliated daycare, apparently found that distinction more of a suggestion than a commandment. On December 16, 2025, Mitchell pleaded guilty to federal tax evasion, capping a scheme that prosecutors say ran for the better part of a decade. The guilty plea marks a formal legal acknowledgment of conduct that federal authorities describe as a systematic betrayal of both his congregation and his civic obligations.

According to the Department of Justice, the conduct in question spanned from 2015 to 2022, a seven-year window during which Mitchell allegedly treated two nonprofit organizations as a personal expense account. The credit cards belonging to his church and its affiliated daycare were used to fund a lifestyle that had little to do with scripture and everything to do with personal indulgence. The charges center on tax evasion, which means Mitchell did not merely misuse funds; he also failed to report the resulting income to federal tax authorities. That dual layer of financial misconduct is what elevated this from an internal church matter to a federal criminal case.

The items charged to the organizations' accounts read less like ministry expenses and more like the browsing history of someone who just discovered a no-limit card. Prosecutors identified men's clothing, thousands of dollars in jewelry, luxury watches, and personal life insurance premiums among the charges run through organizational accounts. Each of these categories represents a personal financial benefit that was never disclosed as taxable income. The breadth of the categories also suggests this was not an isolated lapse in judgment but a practiced habit.

U.S. Attorney Joseph Nocella offered a characterization of Mitchell's conduct that was notable for its directness. Nocella stated that Mitchell treated his organizations' accounts as a personal piggy bank, stealing millions and betraying his congregation. That language, coming from a federal prosecutor in an official announcement, is not rhetorical flourish; it is a legal and moral summary of what the guilty plea establishes. Federal prosecutors do not typically invoke the word "betrayal" in press releases unless they believe the record supports it.

The daycare component of this case deserves particular attention. Mitchell was not only the spiritual leader of a congregation of adults who chose to attend his church; he was also the president of an organization responsible for the care of young children and the financial trust of their families. That dual institutional role placed an especially high fiduciary obligation on Mitchell, one that he appears to have honored mostly in the breach. Families who enrolled their children in the daycare had no reasonable basis to suspect that organizational funds were subsidizing pastoral jewelry purchases.

From a structural standpoint, the scheme reflects a well-documented vulnerability in small nonprofit organizations: governance gaps that allow a single authority figure to exercise unchecked control over financial accounts. When a pastor also serves as a daycare president and faces no meaningful board oversight of credit card expenditures, the conditions for exactly this kind of misconduct are already in place. The IRS and federal prosecutors have long identified such governance structures as risk factors for self-dealing in tax-exempt organizations. Mitchell's case fits that pattern almost point for point.

The tax evasion charge itself carries significant legal weight. Under federal law, tax evasion is a felony that can result in substantial prison time, financial penalties, and restitution obligations. A guilty plea, as opposed to a conviction at trial, typically reflects cooperation with prosecutors and some degree of negotiated resolution, though sentencing remains at the discretion of the court. The full consequences for Mitchell will not be known until sentencing proceedings conclude, but the guilty plea itself closes the door on any claim of innocence.

It is worth pausing on the timeline. Mitchell's conduct, according to prosecutors, began in 2015 and continued through 2022. That is seven years of Sunday sermons, pastoral counseling sessions, and community events occurring in parallel with what federal prosecutors describe as an ongoing theft. The congregation and the daycare families were, in effect, funding a private luxury lifestyle while presumably receiving spiritual guidance and childcare services in return. The asymmetry between what was given and what was taken is considerable.

Mitchell's case joins a depressingly well-populated category of clergy financial misconduct cases in the United States. Religious organizations receive significant tax benefits under federal law, and the expectation embedded in those benefits is that funds will be directed toward charitable and communal purposes. When leaders exploit those structures for personal enrichment, they impose costs not only on their own congregants but on the broader framework of public trust that makes nonprofit tax exemption politically viable. Mitchell did not commit a victimless crime.

The clothing and jewelry purchases, in particular, carry a symbolic weight that is hard to ignore. These are not ambiguous expenses that could be rationalized as ministry-adjacent. A luxury watch does not preach. Men's clothing purchased on a church credit card does not feed the hungry or shelter the homeless. Each item on the prosecution's list is a self-contained argument against Mitchell's fitness for the role he held. The congregation deserved better accounting of its resources, and the children in the daycare program deserved a president who understood the difference between organizational funds and personal discretionary income.

There is, of course, a formal legal process still underway. Mitchell has pleaded guilty, but he has not yet been sentenced. The court will determine the appropriate penalty, taking into account factors including the total financial loss to the organizations, Mitchell's degree of cooperation with prosecutors, and any mitigating circumstances his defense may present. Whatever the outcome of sentencing, the guilty plea itself is a legal and historical fact that will define his legacy in ways no Sunday morning sermon can fully undo.

For the congregation and the daycare families, the more immediate challenge is institutional. Rebuilding financial governance, restoring donor and parent trust, and determining the full scope of the losses are tasks that will outlast the criminal proceedings. Organizations that have experienced this level of leadership betrayal often face years of reputational and financial rehabilitation. The guilty plea is an ending for the prosecution; for the people who trusted Mitchell, it is more complicated than that.

The Department of Justice announcement describes a man who occupied a position of profound communal trust and used it, systematically and for years, to enrich himself at the expense of those he was supposed to serve. That is not a complicated story, morally speaking. It is, however, a sadly recurring one, and the frequency with which it recurs is itself worth examining. Paul Mitchell is the latest entry in a ledger that grows longer with each passing year, a record of spiritual authority converted, without apparent remorse, into personal financial advantage.
